symbols: winged infant — the putto representing angelic presence and heavenly joy in sacred spaces
dynamic flying pose — the lightness and freedom of celestial beings unbound by earthly gravity
description: A gilded wooden sculpture of a plump winged infant (putto) mounted on a green-yellow wall. The figure is carved in a dynamic flying pose with arms and legs extended, as if descending through the air. The wood has a warm golden-brown patina. The carving shows expressive detail in the curly hair, rounded cheeks, and chubby limbs. Thin wire supports are visible holding the figure to the wall.
theological_description: Putti in church decoration evoke the heavenly host that surrounds God's throne. While distinct from the fearsome biblical cherubim described by Ezekiel with four faces and four wings (Ezekiel 10:14), the putto tradition draws on the broader theology of angelic beings who ceaselessly praise God. The psalmist calls upon all creation: 'Praise the Lord from the heavens; praise him in the heights above. Praise him, all his angels' (Psalm 148:1-2). These playful celestial infants populate sacred architecture as visual reminders that worship on earth participates in the eternal liturgy of heaven. Their child-like form may also evoke Christ's teaching: 'Unless you change and become like little children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven' (Matthew 18:3).

scripture_refs.reason: Praise the Lord from the heavens, praise him all his angels — the function of angelic beings in worship
The biblical cherubim from which the putto tradition ultimately derives
Becoming like little children to enter the kingdom — reflected in the child-like angelic form
